title: Getting the China Dimension of USMCA Right
gist: Ahead of the July 1 USMCA review, the author argues the agreement's real stakes are how the US, Canada and Mexico jointly treat China — not the sector choreography dominating headlines. He proposes three deliverables: a shared database of Chinese investment, smarter (not tougher) rules of origin using blockchain tracing, and modernized, trilaterally coordinated investment screening — especially in Mexico.

themes.theme: China as the real USMCA stake

insights.claim: The dimension that will shape North America's security and competitiveness in the USMCA review is how to treat and compete with China — a question crowded out by debate over sector reopenings and whether the deal extends 16 years or slides into annual reviews.
insights.speaker: Antonio Ortiz-Mena
insights.so_what: Reframes the USMCA review as a China-containment negotiation — the variable to watch for North American supply-chain and auto/steel investors.
insights.quote: the dimension that will shape North America’s security and competitiveness: how to treat and compete with China

insights.claim: The three countries currently manage China concerns reactively and individually, and these measures sometimes overshoot — obstructing deeper regional integration instead of countering unfair competition.
insights.speaker: Antonio Ortiz-Mena
insights.so_what: Uncoordinated anti-China measures raise intra-North-American trade barriers — a friction cost for cross-border manufacturers.
insights.quote: they can end up obstructing deeper regional integration instead
insights.locator: The three countries worry about China’s unfair trade practices

insights.claim: Trump's rhetoric and tariff policy have strained allies' trust: Canada's Carney warned of a 'rupture' of the international system while Mexico's Sheinbaum showed restraint and confidence in the negotiations.
insights.speaker: Antonio Ortiz-Mena
insights.so_what: Diverging Canadian vs Mexican postures toward Washington shape the negotiating dynamic and trilateral cohesion risk.
insights.quote: Canada’s Mark Carney warned about the “rupture” of the international system

insights.claim: The three countries are debating Chinese investment without agreeing on its nature and extent, as independent assessments diverge sharply on the stock and flows of Chinese investment in the US and Mexico.
insights.speaker: Antonio Ortiz-Mena
insights.so_what: Absence of a common factual baseline means policy is being set 'partly blind' — a shared database would be the first concrete USMCA deliverable to watch.
insights.quote: they will be designing trade and investment policy partly blind

insights.claim: A shared database would enable coordination toward a common external tariff (or near-equivalent) on selected items like EVs or steel from non-FTA partners, set within WTO limits — a shared perimeter rather than an unfair-trade remedy.
insights.speaker: Antonio Ortiz-Mena
insights.so_what: A North American common external tariff on Chinese EVs/steel would reshape regional auto and metals competitive dynamics.
insights.quote: a common tariff on electric vehicles and/or steel from non-FTA partners, set within WTO limits
insights.locator: The three countries might explore a common external tariff

insights.claim: The author argues the US should eliminate its Section 232 steel and aluminum tariffs on Canada and Mexico — which do little for security since the US can't produce enough on its own — and instead coordinate a shared tariff aimed at subsidized or dumped Chinese steel.
insights.speaker: Antonio Ortiz-Mena
insights.so_what: A push to drop intra-North-American metals tariffs in favor of a China-targeted perimeter is bullish for Canadian/Mexican steel and aluminum exporters if adopted.
insights.quote: taxing two reliable neighbors does little for security
insights.locator: The U.S. currently imposes tariffs on steel and aluminum

insights.claim: USMCA's rules of origin are currently agnostic about the origin of capital and technology, and the US is expected to seek conditions on where capital and technology come from during the review.
insights.speaker: Antonio Ortiz-Mena
insights.so_what: New capital/tech-origin conditions in rules of origin would directly affect Chinese-linked investment in Mexican manufacturing.
insights.quote: the U.S. will seek to change that by attaching conditions on where capital and technology come from
insights.locator: Today, USMCA’s rules of origin are agnostic

insights.claim: Blockchain-based origin tracing now makes verification far cheaper and more reliable than paperwork, so security concerns can be addressed with precision rather than blunt 'tough' measures that impose costs on the region itself.
insights.speaker: Antonio Ortiz-Mena
insights.so_what: Blockchain origin-tracing as a USMCA compliance tool is an emerging trade-tech angle to watch in supply-chain verification.
insights.quote: blockchain-based tracing of the origin of goods, for instance, now makes verification far cheaper and more reliable
insights.locator: This is where being smart beats being tough

insights.claim: The most effective and most neglected fix is modernizing foreign-investment screening — especially in Mexico — and coordinating it trilaterally to address national security around the origin of capital and technology.
insights.speaker: Antonio Ortiz-Mena
insights.so_what: Mexico's screening modernization is the key structural deliverable; its pace signals how serious North America is on China — watch the stalled bilateral working group.
insights.quote: Modernize foreign investment screening—especially in Mexico—so it directly addresses national security
insights.locator: The most effective way to address concerns

insights.claim: A US-Mexico bilateral working group on investment screening that then-Treasury Secretary Yellen requested in late 2023 had still not been established by mid-2026, an inexcusably slow pace.
insights.speaker: Antonio Ortiz-Mena
insights.so_what: The multi-year delay flags low execution capacity on screening — tempers expectations that the July review delivers a coordinated framework quickly.
insights.quote: It is now mid-2026. The pace has been inexcusably slow.
insights.locator: In late 2023, then–Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen

insights.claim: The line between welcome and worrisome Chinese investment is not simple: in May Trump took 17 CEOs including Musk and Huang to meet Chinese authorities, and Geely-owned Volvo Cars operates a major assembly plant in South Carolina.
insights.speaker: Antonio Ortiz-Mena
insights.so_what: Highlights inconsistency in the US stance on Chinese capital — screening policy will be selective, not blanket, affecting how Chinese-linked deals are treated.
insights.quote: Volvo Cars—owned by China’s Geely—operates a major assembly plant in South Carolina
insights.locator: It also requires frankness in both directions
